head_coach.name: Shane Steichen
head_coach.scheme_philosophy: RPO-heavy, run-first foundation with play-action shots; adapts offensive system to quarterback's strengths. 'Throw to score, run to win.' Norv Turner coaching tree via Chargers, later Philadelphia Eagles OC.

offensive_coordinator.name: Jim Bob Cooter
offensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Peyton Manning/Tom Moore concepts, Erhardt-Perkins influences via Detroit/Caldwell; emphasis on quick-game, de-stressing the QB, and power run schemes
offensive_coordinator.notes: Steichen calls plays on game day. Cooter is the game-plan architect and coordinator, building weekly plans. Third season as Colts OC.

defensive_coordinator.name: Lou Anarumo
defensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Secondary-coaching roots (Purdue, Miami Dolphins, Giants DB coach); Kevin Coyle/Bengals zone coverage lineage; self-described 'multiple' schemer — not Belichick or Carroll tree, independently developed approach emphasizing coverage disguise and varied pressure

offense.personnel_tendencies: Primarily 11 personnel (3 WR, 1 TE, 1 RB). Tyler Warren played 899 of 1,075 offensive snaps (84%) as the primary TE in 2025, with Mo Alie-Cox as the second TE at 421 snaps, indicating significant but not dominant 12-personnel usage as a change-of-pace look.
offense.run_scheme: Inside zone and power run option (PRO), with zone read concepts. Steichen blends inside zone RPOs with designed QB power runs. Top-10 rushing offense in yards per game in both 2023 and 2024. Jonathan Taylor led NFL with 323 carries and 1,585 rushing yards in 2025.
offense.pass_concepts: RPO-heavy (approximately 20% of plays were RPOs in Philadelphia, carried to Indianapolis), play-action off run game, quick game and short completions to stay ahead of schedule, vertical shots off play action, screen game including TE screens. Steichen adapts passing concepts to QB — used more quick-hitters with Minshew, more deep shots with Jones.
offense.te_usage: Primary receiving weapon and do-it-all piece. Tyler Warren (1st round, 2025) set franchise rookie TE record with 76 catches for 817 yards and 5 TDs. Warren played inline, flexed to slot, used on screens — described as 'Swiss army knife.' The Colts invested the No. 14 overall pick to get this versatile TE archetype.
offense.rb_usage: Bell cow usage for Jonathan Taylor — led NFL in carries and rushing TDs (18) in 2025. Taylor is primarily a runner; the Colts historically do not heavily target RBs in the passing game. In Steichen's Eagles tenure, RBs finished last in the NFL in targets. Taylor's receiving role is limited to screens and checkdowns, not route-running from the backfield.

defense.base_front: Multiple / nickel-dominant with 4-2-5 as primary package
defense.alignment: Even front (under front) as core alignment, but Anarumo uses a 5-2 'grizzly' front against heavy personnel and has run as few as 7 traditional 4-3 snaps all season. Nickel (4-2-5) is the true base, with the 5-2 used situationally against run-heavy teams.
defense.edge_rush_source: 4-3 style DEs with hands in dirt — Laiatu Latu (weakside/speed rusher, Hendrickson comp) and Kwity Paye (strong side). DEs also drop into coverage in Anarumo's scheme (Latu recorded an interception in coverage in Week 1). DeForest Buckner and Grover Stewart anchor interior.
defense.coverage_base: Multiple / disguise-heavy. Mix of Cover 3, Cover 2, and Cover 4 (Quarters) with week-to-week variation. Anarumo played man coverage ~25% of the time in Cincinnati (near league average). Heavy emphasis on disguising pre-snap looks to confuse QBs. Increasing use of Quarters coverage behind five-man pressures (Tango pressures).
defense.blitz_tendency: moderate-to-heavy (28.6% over 6 years in Cincinnati, but ramped up significantly in Indianapolis — 41.1% blitz rate in Week 1 of 2025). Uses varied pressure packages: Tango pressures, Hot pressures, Creeper concepts. Blitzes safeties and cornerbacks; Mike Hilton led NFL CBs in blitzes under Anarumo in Cincinnati.
defense.lb_role: ILBs (Zaire Franklin, Germaine Pratt) are primarily run/coverage players. Franklin was a league-leading tackler. Anarumo's nickel-heavy approach means only 1-2 LBs on field most snaps — LBs are NOT used as edge rushers. OLB as a pass-rush position does not exist in this scheme.
defense.safety_usage: Split roles: Cam Bynum as free safety/deep coverage ballhawk; Nick Cross as strong safety/box player/blitzer. Cross led Colts in tackles in 2025, logged 16 QB pressures and multiple sacks — Anarumo frequently designs blitzes for Cross off the edge. Bynum patrols deep middle and was signed specifically for Anarumo's coverage-disguise scheme. This is a high-usage hybrid safety system.

te_receiving_boost.explanation: The Colts invested the No. 14 overall pick in Tyler Warren specifically for his receiving versatility. Warren played 84% of offensive snaps as a rookie, caught 76 passes for 817 yards (franchise rookie TE record), and was used inline, in the slot, and on screens. The offense operates primarily from 11 personnel where the TE is essentially a fourth pass-catcher. Steichen's scheme deploys the TE as a move piece in RPO concepts, red zone fades, and play-action targets. Any TE with elite receiving traits — route running, hands, YAC, speed — gets significantly elevated value in this system because the TE is not merely a blocker but a featured weapon.

lb_pass_rush_boost.explanation: Anarumo's defense is nickel-dominant (4-2-5) with DEs as the primary edge rushers in a four-down even front. The Colts had exactly 7 traditional 4-3 snaps (4 DL, 3 LBs) all season. LBs in this system (Franklin, Pratt) are coverage/run-stop players, not pass rushers. Edge rush comes from DEs Latu and Paye plus interior DTs. There is no 3-4 OLB role. An LB with pass rush traits has no positional pathway to edge rushing in Indianapolis. The pressures that do come from non-DL players are designed for safeties and cornerbacks, not LBs.

rb_dual_threat_boost.explanation: The Colts are a run-first offense that historically does not involve RBs in the passing game. Under Steichen in Philadelphia, Eagles RBs finished dead last in the NFL in targets. Jonathan Taylor led the NFL in carries in 2025 but had limited receiving work. PFF noted all three Colts RBs (Taylor, Giddens, Herbert) are 'best suited for early-down work rather than on third downs.' The Colts value a bell-cow runner who can dominate between the tackles, not a receiving back. Dual-threat RB traits provide minimal additional value here.

s_hybrid_boost.explanation: Anarumo's defense creates enormous value for hybrid safeties. Nick Cross plays a strong safety/box/blitzer role — he led the Colts in tackles (69 through 10 games), logged 16 QB pressures, 2.5 sacks, and the 4th-highest run-stop rate among safeties per PFF. Anarumo frequently designs safety blitzes and rotates pre-snap to disguise coverage. Cross' versatility — tackling, blitzing, coverage — is exactly the archetype Anarumo exploits. Cam Bynum operates as the deep coverage safety. Any safety with box ability, blitz skills, tackling physicality, and enough coverage versatility to rotate assignments is extremely valuable in this defense.

scheme_specific_draft_notes: Indianapolis is a PRIME destination for receiving TEs and hybrid safeties, and a poor fit for pass-rushing LBs or dual-threat RBs. The Colts proved this in 2025 by drafting Tyler Warren 14th overall as a move TE and signing Cam Bynum to a $60M deal to pair with box safety Nick Cross. Anarumo's nickel-dominant defense (4-2-5 base, only 7 true 4-3 snaps all season) eliminates any LB→EDGE pathway; edge rush comes exclusively from DEs like Latu and Paye. However, Anarumo's scheme is a goldmine for versatile safeties — Cross' role as a blitzing, tackling SS who also logged 16 QB pressures illustrates how a hybrid safety is valued like a LB/EDGE hybrid in this defense. On offense, Steichen's RPO-heavy, 11-personnel system treats the TE as a featured receiver, not a blocking appendage. Any TE prospect with route-running, hands, and YAC ability should be valued at WR-adjacent prices for Indianapolis. RB value is purely as a between-the-tackles runner — Steichen's offenses consistently rank last in RB receiving targets.
